agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation, trials) with payment validation, retry logic, and tier-specific policies.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees, carrying a validity/failure status."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when a subscriber is charged."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated process of charging a subscriber's payment method to extend their subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Renewal Attempt","definition":"A single try to collect payment during the renewal window. Failed attempts are counted toward cancellation thresholds."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A time window after renewal failure during which the subscription remains active before automatic cancellation (applies to Premium tier)."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A time-limited subscription that grants access without payment, subject to one-per-email constraints."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that grants enhanced cancellation protection via a 14-day grace period instead of the standard 3-strike rule."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription against a known-bad payment method creates a corrupt state: the system would grant access it can never collect revenue for. No business policy change would make it acceptable to knowingly activate service on a broken payment instrument. This guards data consistency between the payment and subscription aggregates.","testable_assertion":"Given a customer with only failed payment methods on file, when they attempt to activate a subscription, then the activation MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST NOT transition to 'active' state."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision balancing retry window against customer experience. A VP could change this to 5 days or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, retry success rate data, or competitive pressure. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this window changed — it is a tunable operational parameter.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, when the current date is exactly 7 days before the billing cycle end date, then the system MUST initiate an automatic renewal charge attempt."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The threshold of 3 attempts is a business policy. Management could decide that 5 attempts or 2 attempts better serves the business based on churn analysis, payment recovery rates, or cost of service during unpaid periods. The number is a configurable policy knob, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an active standard-tier subscription with 2 prior failed renewal attempts, when the 3rd renewal attempt fails, then the subscription MUST be automatically transitioned to 'cancelled' state."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state machine integrity constraint. Allowing a cancelled subscription to return to active would violate the subscription lifecycle's state transition rules, potentially corrupting billing history, audit trails, and revenue recognition. The cancelled state is terminal by design — reactivation would mean the same subscription entity carries two disjoint billing histories, breaking referential integrity of invoices, payments, and usage records tied to that subscription ID.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in 'cancelled' state, when any operation attempts to transition it to 'active' state, then the operation MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST remain in 'cancelled' state."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Both the 14-day duration and the decision to give Premium subscribers preferential treatment are business policy choices. The grace period length could be adjusted to 21 days or 10 days based on Premium tier economics. The company could also decide to apply grace periods to all tiers, or change Premium to a 5-strike rule instead. None of these changes would corrupt system state — they are retention strategy levers.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription whose renewal has failed, when 14 days have NOT yet elapsed since the first failed renewal attempt, then the subscription MUST remain active. When the 14-day grace period expires without a successful payment, then the subscription MUST be automatically cancelled."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and uniqueness constraint. Allowing multiple concurrent active trials per email would represent a corrupt state — it means the deduplication logic has failed, enabling abuse of the trial system. This is analogous to a unique constraint in a database: two active trials for the same email is a data integrity violation, not a policy preference. While a business could decide to allow sequential trials (after one expires), simultaneous active trials represent a system state that should never exist.","testable_assertion":"Given an email address that already has an active free trial subscription, when a new free trial activation is attempted for the same email address, then the activation MUST be rejected and only the original trial MUST remain active."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer whose only payment method on file has a 'failed' status","when":"The customer attempts to activate a new subscription","then":"The system rejects activation with a clear error indicating the payment method must be updated"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on day T","when":"The current date reaches T minus 7 days","then":"The system initiates an automatic renewal charge against the subscriber's payment method"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A standard-tier subscription that has accumulated 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts","when":"The 3rd failure is recorded","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled and the customer is notified"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in 'cancelled' state","when":"Any actor (customer, admin, system) attempts to reactivate it","then":"The operation is rejected; the customer is directed to create a new subscription"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription whose renewal payment has failed","when":"Less than 14 days have elapsed since the first failure","then":"The subscription remains active and the system continues retry attempts"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription whose grace period has expired (14 days since first failure) without successful payment","when":"The grace period clock expires","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An email address with an existing active free trial","when":"A new free trial signup is attempted with the same email","then":"The signup is rejected with a message indicating an active trial already exists"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer has multiple payment methods, some failed and some valid","expected_behavior":"Activation should succeed if at least one non-failed payment method exists on file. BR-1 blocks activation only when there are NO valid payment methods."},{"scenario":"Renewal window overlaps with a plan change (e.g., standard to Premium mid-cycle)","expected_behavior":"The tier at the time of renewal failure determines which cancellation policy applies. If upgraded to Premium before failure, the 14-day grace period applies."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber's payment succeeds on day 13 of the grace period","expected_behavior":"The grace period resets, the subscription remains active, and the next billing cycle begins from the successful payment date."},{"scenario":"Customer cancels voluntarily (not auto-cancelled) — can they reactivate?","expected_behavior":"BR-4 applies uniformly: cancelled is a terminal state regardless of cancellation reason. A new subscription must be created."},{"scenario":"Free trial expires naturally, customer signs up for another trial with the same email","expected_behavior":"This should be ALLOWED — BR-6 prohibits concurrent active trials, not sequential trials. The first trial is no longer active."},{"scenario":"3rd renewal attempt fails but payment processor returns an ambiguous/pending status","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous states should NOT trigger auto-cancellation. Only definitive failure counts. The system should wait for settlement or treat pending as a non-failure until resolved."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber has 3 failed attempts within the 14-day grace period","expected_behavior":"The 3-strike rule does NOT apply to Premium. The grace period clock governs cancellation, not attempt count. Subscription remains active until day 14 expires."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect implementation of BR-1 could lead to revenue leakage by granting access without collectible payment. Misconfigured retry windows (BR-2) directly affect payment recovery rates — industry benchmarks show 7-day windows recover 15-25% of initially failed payments. Premature cancellation of Premium subscribers (BR-5) risks losing highest-LTV customers.","operational":"The 7-day renewal lead time (BR-2) creates a dependency on payment processor retry scheduling. The trial uniqueness constraint (BR-6) requires a reliable, real-time deduplication check that must handle race conditions under concurrent signups. Grace period tracking (BR-5) requires a durable timer/scheduler that survives system restarts.","user":"Customers blocked from activation due to BR-1 need a clear path to update their payment method. Auto-cancellation notifications (BR-3, BR-5) must be timely and actionable. The no-reactivation policy (BR-4) will frustrate customers who expect to resume — clear UX messaging is essential to reduce support burden.","risk":"BR-4 (no reactivation) carries churn risk if competitors allow reactivation. BR-6 (trial uniqueness) can be circumvented with email aliases (user+tag@domain.com) unless normalized. The 3-strike vs. grace period split (BR-3 vs BR-5) adds complexity that could lead to bugs if tier detection is unreliable.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Standard Tier)","impact":"Subject to 3-strike auto-cancellation with no grace period; must create new subscriptions after cancellation."},{"group":"Customers (Premium Tier)","impact":"Receive preferential 14-day grace period, reducing involuntary churn from transient payment issues."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue Team","impact":"Depends on correct renewal timing and retry logic for revenue forecasting and cash flow predictability."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle escalations from customers confused by no-reactivation policy and trial uniqueness rejections."},{"group":"Growth / Marketing","impact":"Trial abuse prevention (BR-6) directly affects customer acquisition cost and trial conversion metrics."}]},"confidence":{"level":82,"reasoning":"The spec is unusually clear and internally consistent. Each rule is specific and testable. Minor ambiguities exist around edge cases (e.g., what constitutes a 'failed' payment method, how Premium grace period interacts with retry attempts, email alias normalization for trials) but the core rules are well-defined and non-contradictory.","high_confidence_areas":["Subscription lifecycle state machine (activation, cancellation terminal state)","Trial uniqueness constraint classification as invariant","Invariant vs. policy distinction for the 3-strike threshold and 7-day window"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'failed payment method' means the method itself is flagged vs. the last transaction failed","How the Premium grace period timer starts — on first failure or on the renewal date","Email normalization rules for trial uniqueness (aliases, dots, case sensitivity)"],"assumptions":["The 14-day grace period for Premium completely replaces the 3-strike rule rather than augmenting it","'Failed payment method' refers to a payment method whose status is flagged as failed/invalid, not merely one whose last charge was declined","Sequential free trials with the same email are permitted; only concurrent active trials are prohibited"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says Premium gets a '14-day grace period instead of the 3-strike rule.' It is unclear whether Premium subscribers can ALSO be cancelled after N failed attempts within the 14 days, or if the grace period is purely time-based regardless of attempt count.","affected_stakeholders":["Premium Tier Customers","Finance Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Grace period is purely time-based — no attempt limit for Premium","B: Grace period adds a time dimension but the 3-strike rule still applies within it"],"recommendation":"Option A — the word 'instead of' implies full replacement, not augmentation. Premium subscribers should only be cancelled when the 14-day clock expires."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not define what constitutes a 'failed payment method on file' for BR-1. Is it a payment method entity with a persisted failure flag, or does it mean the most recent charge attempt against that method failed?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Engineering","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Payment method has an explicit status field (valid/failed/expired) checked at activation","B: System checks whether the last transaction on the method was declined"],"recommendation":"Option A — a persisted status field is more reliable and auditable. Transaction-level checks are ephemeral and may not reflect the current state of the instrument."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
